title: Needle-felted stag beetle
subtitle: A glossy black stag beetle in merino with mandible wire and wire legs
excerpt: Build a male stag beetle in black and chestnut merino, with wire-wrapped antler mandibles and six wire legs. The finished beetle is about 7 cm long and is one of the more technically involved small-scale needle-felted insects.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Insect forms with multiple wire appendages are a standard intermediate British needle-felting project; the wire-leg technique follows standard guild workshop method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
A wire frame inside or attached to a needle-felted piece. For an insect, wire legs and mandibles provide structure that roving alone cannot maintain.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for needle felting.

content.text: has three distinct sections: head, thorax, and abdomen. The projecting mandibles and six legs all need an
. The mandibles are chestnut brown to match the colouration of a real male stag beetle; the rest of the body is glossy black achieved by needling the surface very densely.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Craft wire (0.4 mm), for legs and mandibles

content.text: Step 1: Build the three body sections

Needle felt three separate pieces from black roving: a small rounded head (1.5 cm), a flat oval thorax (2 cm), and a larger, slightly ridged abdomen (3.5 cm). Work each section to a very high density so the surface is nearly smooth and the black colour is deep. Join the three sections by needling the contact surfaces together, working from several angles.
Step 2: Make and attach the legs

Cut six 7 cm lengths of wire. Wrap each in a thin layer of black roving and needle along the length to secure. Bend each leg into a gentle S-curve to mimic beetle leg jointing. Insert and needle the top 1.5 cm of each leg into the underside of the thorax, three on each side, spacing them evenly.

Cut two 5 cm lengths of wire. Wrap in chestnut brown roving and needle to secure. Bend each into a curved tine with a small upward point near the tip. Attach each mandible to the front of the head by threading the base through the head and folding it back on itself inside the head mass, then needle felt over the base to lock it.

Work over the entire body surface with a fine needle to create an even, dense surface. The stag beetle has a naturally shiny carapace; very dense needling with a fine gauge needle produces the closest approximation. Add very small black bumps at the thorax-abdomen join to suggest the wing cases.

items.problem: The body sections separate after joining
items.cause: Insufficient needling at the join points between sections
items.fix: Work the join from all angles, needling down into both sections simultaneously. Add a small wisp of roving over the join and needle it in to bridge the gap.

items.problem: The legs all point in the same direction
items.cause: The legs were inserted at the same angle rather than varying their position
items.fix: Space the six leg attachment points evenly along the thorax. Point the front pair forward, the middle pair out to the sides, and the back pair backward. Each leg should be at a different angle.
